text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

ATTENDING: Gynecologic Oncology
CONSULTING SERVICE: Gynecologic Oncology
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: Primary Gynecology
REASON FOR CONSULTATION: New diagnosis of advanced ovarian malignancy, treatment planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman of French-Canadian descent referred to Gynecologic Oncology for evaluation and management of newly diagnosed high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC. She presents today accompanied by her husband.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman (natural menopause at age 50) who presented approximately 3 months ago with progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 pounds, and a sense of pelvic pressure and heaviness. She initially attributed her symptoms to menopausal changes and dietary indiscretion. Her primary gynecologist noted bilateral adnexal masses on pelvic examination and referred her for further workup.

Serum tumor markers at that time were markedly elevated: CA-125 1,847 U/mL and HE4 312 pmol/L, with a ROMA score in the high-risk range. Pelvic ultrasound and subsequent CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast confirmed bilateral complex adnexal masses (right 8.2 cm, left 5.6 cm) with thick septations, solid components, and papillary projections. Imaging further demonstrated large-volume ascites, omental caking (approximately 12 x 8 cm), extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with implants on the diaphragm bilaterally (right-sided dominant, up to 1.8 cm), pelvic peritoneum, paracolic gutters, and liver surface (largest implant 0.9 cm). Pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy was identified (right external iliac node 1.4 cm, left internal iliac 1.2 cm, para-aortic up to 1.1 cm). No evidence of distant hematogenous metastases; no pleural effusions.

Diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal and omental biopsy was performed. Pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma with characteristic papillary architecture, slit-like glandular spaces, psammoma bodies, and marked nuclear atypia. Immunoprofile: WT1 positive (diffuse strong), PAX8 positive, p53 aberrant (overexpression pattern, consistent with TP53 missense mutation), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, ER positive (30%), PR negative. Ki-67 75%. Diagnosis: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, consistent with primary ovarian/fallopian tube origin, FIGO stage IIIC.

Germline and somatic NGS was subsequently sent given the morphologic and IHC profile. Germline testing revealed a BRCA1 pathogenic variant: c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756Profs*74), the so-called 5382insC Eastern European/French-Canadian founder mutation, classified as pathogenic per ACMG/AMP criteria. Somatic tumor profiling confirmed TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%), BRCA1 somatic LOH, RB1 truncating mutation (p.R358*, VAF 22%), and CCNE1 amplification (copy number ~8). HRD score 56 (above the 42 threshold, HRD-positive). TMB 4.2 mut/Mb (low). MSS. PD-L1 TPS 5%.

Patient was referred to us for surgical and medical oncology management.

She has no prior history of malignancy. She denies prior use of oral contraceptives for a prolonged duration. She has not undergone prior pelvic or abdominal surgery. No prior chemotherapy or radiation.

At today's visit she reports the abdominal distension remains significant and uncomfortable. She has mild dyspnea on exertion with exertion (climbing more than one flight of stairs), which she attributes to elevated diaphragm from ascites. No pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, or frank orthopnea. She continues to lose weight slowly. Appetite is poor. Bowel and bladder function intact, though she notes urinary frequency. No significant vaginal bleeding.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Positive for fatigue, unintentional weight loss (~12 lbs over 3 months), anorexia/early satiety.
Respiratory: Mild exertional dyspnea; no cough, hemoptysis, pleuritis.
Gastrointestinal: Abdominal distension, bloating, early satiety; no hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, diarrhea, or obstruction symptoms. Bowel function otherwise normal.
Genitourinary: Urinary frequency; no hematuria, dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain, no arthralgias.
Neurological: No focal deficits, headache, or vision changes.
Dermatologic: No skin changes, rashes.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Menopausal hot flashes (moderate) and vaginal dryness; not on hormone replacement therapy.
Psychiatric: Anxiety and distress regarding new diagnosis, appropriate.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (newly diagnosed, as above)
2. Hypercholesterolemia (diet-controlled)
3. Menopausal vasomotor symptoms
4. No prior malignancy
5. No prior pelvic surgery

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
1. Appendectomy as a child (remote)
2. Cesarean section x2 (approximately 20-25 years prior)

FAMILY HISTORY
- Mother: Ovarian cancer, diagnosed age 54, died age 58
- Maternal aunt (mother's sister): Breast cancer, diagnosed age 44
- Father: Coronary artery disease
- No known colorectal, endometrial, pancreatic, or prostate cancer in family
- Pedigree is strongly suggestive of BRCA1/2-related hereditary breast-ovarian cancer syndrome; now confirmed by germline testing (BRCA1 5382insC)

SOCIAL HISTORY
- Occupation: High school librarian, full-time
- Marital status: Married, lives with husband; two adult children (daughter age 24, son age 21)
- Tobacco: Never smoker
- Alcohol: Occasional (1-2 drinks/week)
- Illicit substances: Denies
- Exercise: Walks 30 minutes daily prior to illness onset; currently limited by fatigue and abdominal discomfort
- Nutritional status: Reduced intake over past 3 months; currently managing early satiety with small frequent meals
- Support system: Strong; husband and adult children present and engaged

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies (NKDA)
- No known food or environmental allergies

MEDICATIONS
1. Atorvastatin 10 mg PO daily (hypercholesterolemia)
2. Calcium carbonate 1200 mg PO daily with vitamin D 1000 IU (post-menopausal bone health)
3. Multivitamin once daily
4. Ibuprofen PRN (patient counseled to discontinue given upcoming surgical planning)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, RR 16, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 97% on room air
Height: 166 cm, Weight: 72 kg, BMI: 26.1 kg/m2
General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing but mildly distressed by abdominal discomfort. Appropriate affect. No acute distress at rest.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae pink. No supraclavicular or cervical lymphadenopathy.
Neck: Supple. No thyromegaly. No JVD.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally with mildly diminished breath sounds at the right base. No dullness to percussion consistent with clinically significant pleural effusion.
Abdomen: Markedly distended. Fluid wave present consistent with large-volume ascites. Diffuse mild tenderness to deep palpation without peritoneal signs. Omental thickening palpable in mid-abdomen (omental cake palpable as a firm irregular band across the mid-epigastrium and periumbilical region). No palpable hepatosplenomegaly above the limits imposed by ascites. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness. Good peripheral pulses.
Pelvic exam: External genitalia normal. Vaginal mucosa atrophic (consistent with post-menopausal estrogen deficiency). No vaginal or cervical lesions. Bimanual palpation confirms bilateral irregular adnexal masses - right approximately 8-9 cm, left approximately 5-6 cm, firm, nodular, fixed. Uterus difficult to assess due to adnexal masses. Rectovaginal exam: nodular implants palpable along the cul-de-sac and rectovaginal septum.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. CN II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact. Gait normal.
Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular adenopathy.
Skin: No rashes, jaundice, or petechiae.

Performance Status: ECOG 1

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 7.8 K/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, likely related to malignancy/chronic disease)
- Platelets: 398 K/uL (mildly elevated, likely reactive)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60), BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL, ALT: 22 U/L, AST: 18 U/L, Alk Phos: 88 U/L
- Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with malnutritional state/ascites)

Tumor Markers:
- CA-125: 1,847 U/mL (markedly elevated; normal <35 U/mL)
- HE4: 312 pmol/L (elevated)
- ROMA score: High risk

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal), PTT 28 sec (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (staging): Bilateral complex ovarian masses (right 8.2 cm, left 5.6 cm) with solid/cystic components and papillary architecture. Large-volume ascites. Omental caking (~12 x 8 cm). Diaphragmatic implants bilaterally, right-sided dominant (up to 1.8 cm). Implants along pelvic peritoneum and paracolic gutters. Liver surface nodularity (up to 0.9 cm; no parenchymal involvement). Pelvic and para-aortic lymph nodes enlarged (see above). No pleural effusions. No distant metastatic disease identified. Overall picture consistent with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma.

Molecular/Genomic: NGS (germline + somatic) notable for germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (5382insC) PATHOGENIC; somatic TP53 p.R248W, BRCA1 LOH, RB1 truncating, CCNE1 amplification; HRD-positive (score 56); TMB-low; MSS.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Delacroix is a 52-year-old postmenopausal woman with newly confirmed FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary/fallopian tube, with germline BRCA1 5382insC pathogenic variant and HRD-positive tumor. Her presentation is classic: bilateral adnexal masses, massive ascites, omental caking, peritoneal carcinomatosis, retroperitoneal nodal involvement, and a markedly elevated CA-125.

I have had an extensive discussion with Ms. Delacroix and her husband today. We reviewed the diagnosis, the implications of the germline BRCA1 finding, the treatment strategy, and the goals of therapy. The patient is understandably distressed but engaged and asking thoughtful questions.

1. DIAGNOSIS: FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma, ovary/fallopian tube origin
   - Confirmed by histology (biopsy from diagnostic laparoscopy): WT1+, PAX8+, p53 aberrant, CK7+, ER 30%+
   - Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC) confirmed - significant implications for treatment selection (platinum/PARP sensitivity), surveillance, and family cascade testing
   - HRD-positive tumor (score 56) further supports PARP inhibitor sensitivity

2. SURGICAL PLAN - PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS):
   - Given patient's ECOG 1 performance status, absence of significant comorbidities, and imaging assessment suggesting potentially resectable disease, patient is an appropriate candidate for upfront primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS).
   - Goal: R0 resection (no visible residual disease), which is strongly associated with improved overall survival in advanced HGSOC.
   - Planned procedure: Exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, infracolic omentectomy, stripping of diaphragmatic peritoneum, resection of pelvic peritoneal implants, appendectomy, and excision of any additional implants as dictated by intraoperative findings.
   - Surgical risks discussed at length: general anesthetic risks, bleeding, infection, wound dehiscence, venous thromboembolism, ureteral injury, bowel injury (possible bowel resection with or without ostomy depending on intraoperative findings), bladder injury, conversion to open procedure if not already planned open approach, incomplete cytoreduction if disease is more extensive than anticipated intraoperatively. Patient verbalized understanding.
   - Patient consented to proceed with PDS.
   - Pre-operative bowel preparation and VTE prophylaxis will be arranged.
   - Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation requested.
   - Nutrition consult requested given albumin 3.4 and recent weight loss; will attempt nutritional optimization prior to surgery.

3. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY (POST-OPERATIVE PLAN):
   - Following surgery, standard of care adjuvant chemotherapy: Carboplatin AUC 6 / Paclitaxel (Taxol) 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days x 6 cycles
   - Chemotherapy to begin approximately 4-6 weeks post-operatively once patient has adequately recovered
   - Anti-emetic prophylaxis, growth factor support per institutional protocol as needed
   - Discussed common side effects: hematologic toxicity (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia), peripheral neuropathy (cumulative paclitaxel toxicity - a concern to monitor closely), alopecia, nausea/vomiting, fatigue, nephrotoxicity (carboplatin-related), hypersensitivity reactions

4. MAINTENANCE THERAPY (PARP INHIBITOR - DEFERRED DECISION POST-CHEMO):
   - Given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and HRD-positive status, patient is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy following completion of platinum-based chemotherapy, contingent on achieving at least a partial response.
   - Options to be discussed at completion of chemotherapy: niraparib (Zejula) or olaparib (Lynparza) - both FDA-approved in the first-line maintenance setting for BRCA-mutant/HRD-positive advanced ovarian cancer.
   - Will defer definitive maintenance therapy discussion until end of chemotherapy, once response is confirmed.

5. GERMLINE BRCA1 - GENETIC COUNSELING AND FAMILY CASCADE TESTING:
   - Referral placed to Certified Genetic Counselor for formal genetic counseling visit.
   - Patient counseled today on the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern of BRCA1 pathogenic variants: ~50% chance of transmission to each first-degree relative.
   - Her adult children (daughter age 24, son age 21) should be offered cascade testing.
   - Patient's siblings, if any, should also be offered testing.
   - Breast surveillance for BRCA1 carriers: annual mammography + breast MRI per NCCN guidelines (risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy already effectively accomplished as part of PDS).

6. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   - Social work referral placed (psychosocial support, coping resources, cancer support groups).
   - Nutrition consult requested.
   - Physical therapy referral to be placed post-operatively for prehabilitation/rehabilitation.
   - Advance care planning discussion initiated - patient will complete health care proxy documents prior to surgical admission.
   - Ibuprofen discontinued given planned surgery and risk of perioperative bleeding.

7. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Surgical date to be coordinated with OR scheduling within the next 1-2 weeks.
   - Patient to return for pre-operative visit.
   - Repeat CA-125 at time of post-operative visit to establish post-surgical baseline.

Time spent with patient and family in this consultation: 75 minutes (including review of records, examination, counseling, and care coordination).

All questions answered. Patient and husband express understanding of diagnosis and plan and wish to proceed.

_____________________________
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
Gynecologic Oncology Service
